item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this may not be some super sp00py / grotesque big baddie like so much of your other stuff, and yes, it's pretty purple, but I love it when people use something like horror as a vehicle for another, unexpected phenomenon. like the babadook, for instance. grief is a monster that you have to feed sometimes, that you just have to find a way to coexist with.

my dad (technically my adoptive granddad, but he was my dad, both of my children have his last name although I do not) died 22 years ago. for the longest time, I didn't cry. I was only 11, but I felt obligated to be strong for my family. and very shortly thereafter, my grandmother got cancer, and I had to provide her hospice care — no time to mourn. after she died, I fled from my emotions through self destruction. 22 years later, I cry for him multiple times a week. "acceptance" is for other people, not me; and I'm ok with that. I miss him, and I'm ok with missing him. it hurts, but I don't want to stop feeling that pain, if that makes sense. that pain I spent so many years trying to stuff down and starve and slice out of myself. I've finally learned how to just sit with that pain, face it, and let it wash over me. he's worth missing, he's worth that pain. sometimes the pain is worth it. sometimes it's better to stop running.

I didn't expect Narvaez's note at the end. I already identify strongly with him for other reasons, heh. does he have a tag yet?

anyway, I realize this is a slightly contentious piece, but for personal reasons, it really spoke to me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is especially sad taking into account that i know how Dr albert cronenberg will end up :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read this tonight, definitely one of my favorites. I have a preoccupation with death myself, thinking about it too often, about how my time is limited and how I need to do more. It hinders me from enjoying things sometimes. The interviews, especially the final interview, are beautifully done. Thanks for writing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. There's an implied history of the item, the methodical testing to determine its anomalous nature, the colorful interviews, and a final addendum stating that the Foundation is putting this SCP to an actual use.

+1

item.title: This makes me feel... good.
item.content:encoded:
Being a person suffering from depression, This theme touches my soul.
I love the writing, the tests. Doctor Calixto Narvaez…
And I would love to see a picture of a dead lamprey in this text, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of those skips that you feel guilty to downvote. Something about the text and reactions to it make my mind go "What's wrong? Why don't you like it? It's clearly perfect." For a SCP exists to purely to evoke emotion from me, I feel dull. Admittedly, some of the purple prose tickled something somewhere within in me, but rereading it only showed me how, well, absurd it was. The ending especially so. It felt like it was supposed to be some emotional climax, but it's conceptually separated from the the rest of the article, and feels jarring as a result.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking 'too purple, and cluttered. The item isn't that interesting either…' then I read the ending, and saw it in context. I like the idea they're using this item the way they seem to be using it, makes a lot of sense!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have hit the nail on the head with my thoughts on this one. That sentence makes no sense. I like this scip, and agree with what you said about it.

Meta, I'd totally upvote this twice if I could. I'm going to have to devote a brain cell to remembering "2737" and hopefully remember why I've done that, because this is a thing I need to remember to show people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great SCP! I especially like how methodical the testing process in the log was, establishing one thing after another step by step.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never heard of Bertrand Russel. Anyways thanks

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe the equations were made by Bertrand Russel. But struggling to find them now. It had to do with something especially complex. I'll update this when I find it.

Got it! http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Russell%27s_paradox

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey: what website did you get the math Memetic kill agent from? I tried opening the image using right click, but doesn't say the website name. What did you search up in the search bar?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Darnit. D:
fucking metaphysician

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sort of Frankensteined together some equations related to imaginary numbers (if I recall correctly, did that part months ago).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could be a bullshit equation, the point isn't to be mathematically correct it's to be a memetic inoculation agent. Maybe the fact that it's fucked and doesn't work is what causes the right synaptic flare-ups that are the memetic counter-trigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love clever SCPs the foundation makes use of, not fucking cloning trees or pills wot cure every illness 'coz magic innit? Or super guns or monsters, just quirky things that they can find uses for.

These fuckers work hard and die harder, they need a few breaks here and there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I could receive a warning if I used offensive words.
And yes it wasted my test of math. That wasn't really a joke, it's just my bad english. But you're right rape jokes are bad, I'm going to use a different word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am seeking to repair my mistakes. Most related to deleted large chunks and patchworking the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The dead. All of them. It hurts to look. [closes eyes] And I still see them. I feel the knife enter. It's cold. Like a piece of ice.

Just following orders. With every death a world dies with it. How many worlds did you kill? It wasn't out of hate. Just fresh materials to work with. How many did you destroy to find what you needed?

I prayed over his jaundiced husk - invoking every saint I could recall, and a few I likely made up. Was he conscious? I don't know. He writhed as if trapped within a nightmare. They bound him to the bed with leather straps so he wouldn't roll to the floor. The room was filled with bedpans of shit and piss and blood. They injected him with opiates – enough to numb reality.

The interviews are all really overwrought and eventually say nothing that's particularly interesting from a philosophical or human perspective that hasn't been done better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You r***d my test of math with that memetic counter agent

From a non-staff position, this is a really shitty thing to say. If you're really going to censor the particular word you're going for (and there's honestly no other word that fits there in that context except for one), then just use a different word. Rape jokes are shitty, and the fact that you try to be clever about it by censoring also comes off as bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding. It failed to evoke any real feeling from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed. The interviews especially seemed to lack any sense of escalation or pacing and just went straight to the person affected preaching directly to the reader, and there were some things that seemed like random non-sequitors like that person dying of a heart attack. I thought the interviews were pretty terrible and didn't sound like anything human beings would actually say, even if they were affected by a fish that made them sad.

For an article that's trying to talk about the human condition, there seemed to be very little in the way of actual humanity in the portions where humans interacted with each other. The final note was okay, but when coupled with the preachiness and hamminess of the interviews that precede it, it falls flat.

I was totally on board with the idea through the description and most of the test log. I was waiting for the other shoe to drop that would make the thing interesting, and instead I got a bunch of people preaching and going on about philosophy in a way that no human being talks like. Instead of invoking any emotion in me, it just made me realize how unrealistic and wooden the dialogue was. There was no escalation or tension in the conversations, they all follow the same formula.

Person 1: sees card, talks about some oblique death reference

Interviewer: Tries to build on that

Person 1: Cuts them off and starts ranting about philosophy and death and how they need to be empathetic and blah blah blah.

It's very uninteresting. There's three interviews with identical structures and no interesting or different content. There might as well just be one interview. Especially the last one, which is a big wall of text that doesn't really say anything at all that wasn't said in the first interview and regurgitated in the second one.

In fact, this line in the third test log really renders all that overwrought stuff in the interviews irrelevant:

Subject describes the loss of their father at a young age and how they were never properly mourned.

Interview 1: Subject described a murder victim and how they regret doing that now.

Interview 2: Person is upset over waste of life, regrets that life being wasted.

Interview 3: I miss my dad.

A more effective article in this vein might be soup from dad, which has some effective moments of people having different soup flavors lead to a final message about fathers and stuff. It's much, much more effective than this article, because it uses pacing and subtlety to draw the reader in. This article has none of that. That's why I downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little bothered by the tests, as Dr Hysteria mentioned, but mostly I'm just kind of confused as to the purpose of this. I thought it might be some kind of meta-commentary (an SCP that gives people the anomaly of empathy, which the notoriously cold Foundation lacks), but I'm not really sure. I'm gonna fall into the "did nothing for me" crowd and no-vote.

Though I would appreciate an explanation of the anti-meme, as it's been a while since I did logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let x be the set of x1 to xt
Let R be the subset of x where, for each element of x, it is true that xwhatever < y. x should not be a null set.
Add by matrix addition the set xt>y (where the above statement is true) and the set xt>y (where the above statement is false).
If it is true that set xt>y is a null set, then it is true that the set R is a subset of x (but R does not equal x).

I more or less tried to directly translate it; I didn't evaluate it, so think of it as Google Translate for math.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. That means a lot to me. And thanks for the kind words from many others here:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
X equals.. some stuff?
R equals some other stuff.
X with T is greater than Y which is equal to circle with line through..
But then circle with cross inside says that X with T with greater than Y but not circle with line through?
However, if X with T is greater than Y but equal to circle with line through then R is sideways U with X.

Wat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the equation:

$\large x := \{x_1,..., x_t\}$
$\large R := \{x_t|{\forall t<y, x_{t} \neq \varnothing }\}$
$\large x_{t > y}=\varnothing\quad\oplus \quad x_{t > y} \neq \varnothing \\$
$\large \text{If } \exists x_{t > y}=\varnothing \Rightarrow R \subset x$

Aye. There's the rub

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. Let me see if I can grab an easier to understand translation of Plutarch. Also, in the case of Dr. Yi - he is the personal assistant to Dr. Calixto Narváez more or less. I realized a lot of things would seem awkward without knowing exactly what I had in mind. The interviewers undergo the same process - I'll see if I can make that more obvious in say, the special containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gorgeous.

I have two suggestions:

First, I think that the way the risk to the interviewer is managed is not sufficiently addressed. It is a bit jarring to see Dr. Yi conduct an interview after experiencing one from the other side. I get that some counter-memetics are helpful, but it seems that there would be a particular risk of relapse that requires additional mitigation.

Second, I think that particular translation from Plutarch is a little hard to follow grammatically (I see it is rendered exactly that way on Wikipedia, but I find myself going "who said what to who now?" about three times before I get it). There are other versions of that passage around, so you would be forgiven for rendering it even a little more plainly.

These feels aren't for everyone, I guess - but catharsis works best after tragedy. I could see how this might not move someone if it's the first article they read in a sitting. My advice to them is to this: start with an appetizer of Hiccup Jacet and an entrée of A Slumber did my Spirit Seal. Then try this for dessert.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the best skips I've ever read.
You've outdone yourself, Metaphysician.
To those who don't get anything out of this: you'll understand some day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You wasted my test of math with that memetic counter agent, make it more complex, due I understood all symbols, I was confused.
Otherwise, I like your style of writing and the effects:

  • Increased empathy (both emotional and cognitive)
  • Major depressive disorder

No Vote

EDIT: no censoring anymore

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thing is with this one, simply being aware of it triggers the effect. They could not research the item without being aware of it. Ergo, they are now test subjects themselves out of necessity as long as they are researching that item. I'd imagine that they have to do this with every SCP of this sort, and in this case the effect is fairly mild to begin with: it makes you question your existence. As noted, that's a positive thing, ultimately, once you come to terms with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry, it's not really doing much for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seemed preachy and narmy to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I had a problem with was the fact that they experimented on the doctors managing SCP-2737. Why would the Foundation allow able-bodied employees to endure themselves to this entity that makes them question their existence? Why would the ethics committee allow it? I know in the end it's actually helping them, but did the researchers know this? No.

E: I'm down voting this because the illogical testing is too hard to ignore, and the long dialogue (while good) didn't do anything for me. It tried to get across this interesting idea without explaining it well enough in the article. Also, I'm pretty much agreeing with what Famine stated below me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meta, you just wrote the first psychology-oriented skip I've ever read thinking it deserves more than a measly upvote. It is perfect on every side, and every side is perfect.

Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautiful. Well done, Metaphysician. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. To wit:

(1)
\begin{align} \large f(): f(n) =  {p_1}^{a_1}{p_2}^{a_2} \dotsb {p_k}^{a_k} , \forall p  \neg \exists a,b :  a>1,  b>1, p=ab \end{align}

Being serious for a moment, I think this is beautiful:

"If you are reading this then your treatment has already begun. The inoculation merely delayed the memetic agent, allowing you read this document and be better prepared for the experience. SCP-2737 exposure has been shown to outperform psychotherapy and medication in the alleviation of post-traumatic stress disorder, major depressive disorder, secondary traumatic stress, and generalized anxiety disorder. Through decades of research we have fine tuned the therapeutic process. By the end of this session you will likely feel as though a burden has been lifted, with no recollection of this document and its associated experience.

There is an undeserved stigma associated with mental health and its management. Through SCP-2737 treatment you will not be judged, analyzed, and no one will tell you how to live your life. Think to yourself or speak out loud - a lamprey that died 2,000 years ago makes for a surprisingly sympathetic listener.

Today you will cry. You will mourn. You will remember all you have ever lost.

And through this, you will begin to heal."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am sorry for your loss. I first began writing this - trying to get out the feelings of my own loss almost 10 years ago. Specifically, my own fears and experiences spoken through the character of Dr. Calixto Narváez. This was a bit of a personal catharsis, but didn't realize it might effect others in a similar way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this SCP. Definitely worth rereading. Recently one of my oldest friends passed away and I really appreciate this. I think it helps. Thanks for making this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will try to clean this up. I attempted to focus the purple on people's reactions, not in the documentation - I imagine it to be an intense feeling that is difficult to describe. Like tapping into the force for the first time ;p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Took me a reread or two to comprehend, but I quite like it. You might want to tidy up some of the sentences; for example, when you're describing the urn and how it's from 2nd century Rome, you should probably replace "It" with "The urn", since at first I thought you were referring to the lamprey being manufactured in Rome. The first sentence of the discovery paragraph also reads a bit awkardly. Overall, it got pretty purple in some places, and was difficult to wrap my head around, but something about the idea of some ancient Roman's lamprey helping people come to terms with their fears hits just the right notes for me. Also, some of the dialogue kind of reminded me of Synecdoche, New York, which is always a plus in my book.
"We're all hurtling towards death, yet here we are, for the moment, alive. Each of us knowing we're going to die. Each of us secretly believing we won't."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for pointing that out. Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It helps in the healing process but is not a panacea. A person is not magically cured of these things but the object proves a more effective tool of alleviation than psychotherapy and medication. The point of a limited exposure is important and did require decades of tests to figure out just what they could do with it. It results in a process of catharsis - an experience that is difficult to translate. It is an aid, not a cure. Just comparatively effective enough to warrant its use.

The mindset during is use is very different - one is able to look at themselves and the world with much greater clarity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo. A couple times you say "giving" when I think you mean "given." That being said, I'm digging this. Excellent as always, Metadoc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I read this now, the more I question the final note and the role it plays.

+ show block
– hide block

This basically could be oversimplified and boiled down to something that plays therapy and heals you of stuff that's traumatized you in the past. Which… which I'm not sure I like the idea of too much. I think the fact that the note at the end drives this home makes me question this, and it wasn't something I thought about until more.

item.title: Math protects you from memes
item.content:encoded: On a serious note, this was very well done. Having seen earlier versions on this as a draft in chat, I didn't see the final addendum coming at all (having also not seen later versions). Have my upvote, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The trend of myself upvoting Metaphysician articles continues.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2737 is a dead lamprey.

And this is an upvote.
Like I said, a bit purple, but I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have attempted something different from my previous stuff. I am incredibly nervous.

"I know not how oft this Crassus with his lamprey enters my mind as a mirrored image of my Self, reflected across the abyss of centuries."
Philip, Lord Chandos, younger son of the Earl of Bath, in a letter to Francis Bacon

And the point of the quote - Domitius mocks Crassus for mourning the death of his pet. Crassus counters, pointing out that he may be mournful but at least he can feel emotion - unlike Domitius who buried three wives without once shedding a tear for them.
